text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 56-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma (Stage IV, M1c), primary site upper back. Prior therapy: dabra/tram x 8 months with radiographic progression and LDH rise. Subsequently initiated Yervoy/Opdivo combination immunotherapy; Yervoy permanently discontinued following grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 3 (approximately 9 weeks into combination therapy). Opdivo resumed as single-agent maintenance therapy approximately 4 months after immunotherapy initiation following corticosteroid taper and clinical resolution of colitis. Restaging CT performed approximately 5 months after initial immunotherapy start to assess treatment response on Opdivo monotherapy. Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at time of confirmed progression on targeted therapy (pre-immunotherapy baseline, approximately 5 months prior); prior restaging CT approximately 3 months prior to this examination.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following administration of 90 mL Omnipaque 350 IV contrast at standard injection rate. Arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions of the abdomen and pelvis were performed. Multiplanar reformations reviewed. DLP: 1,842 mGy-cm.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are well-aerated without focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules are identified. The central airways are patent without endobronchial lesions. No new pulmonary parenchymal abnormalities compared with prior study.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are identified. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The visualized cardiac chambers appear unremarkable.

Axillary regions: Right axilla: There is a residual, notably diminished soft tissue nodal conglomerate in the right axilla. The dominant nodal mass now measures approximately 1.6 x 1.3 cm in its greatest axial dimensions, representing a substantial decrease compared with the pre-immunotherapy baseline measurement of 3.4 x 2.9 cm and a reduction from 3.4 cm at the most recent prior restaging. The previously described central necrosis is no longer apparent on the current examination; the residual tissue appears heterogeneous but less necrotic. Left axilla: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes identified bilaterally.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No chest wall masses or suspicious soft tissue lesions. Skin and subcutaneous tissues of the chest and trunk appear without discrete nodular lesions. On careful review, there are no measurable subcutaneous nodules identified in the thoracic soft tissues; this represents a marked improvement compared with the pre-immunotherapy baseline when multiple subcutaneous nodules on the trunk measured 0.8 to 1.4 cm.

Bony thorax: No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified on soft tissue and bone windows. No pathologic fracture.

Pleurae: No pleural effusion or pleural thickening.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth contour. On portal venous phase, there are residual hypodense foci scattered throughout the hepatic parenchyma, but these have markedly decreased in number and size compared with the pre-immunotherapy baseline and are substantially smaller than on the prior restaging CT:

- Segment VII lesion (previously the dominant hepatic target lesion): A residual hypodense focus measures approximately 1.2 x 0.9 cm. This represents a dramatic reduction compared with the pre-immunotherapy baseline measurement of 2.8 x 2.4 cm, which itself had increased from a nadir of 1.0 cm on dabra/Mekinist before targeted therapy resistance. The lesion no longer demonstrates the peripheral nodular enhancement pattern previously associated with melanoma metastasis; the residual area appears hypodense and poorly marginated, possibly representing a combination of treatment-related fibrosis, necrotic residual, and/or viable tumor.

- Segment II lesion: A residual hypodense focus measuring approximately 0.7 x 0.6 cm. Compared with the pre-immunotherapy baseline of 1.6 x 1.4 cm, this is markedly smaller. Compared with the prior CT (approximately 3 months ago), this lesion has decreased from 0.7 cm (stable). Enhancement is minimal and indistinct.

- Segment IV lesion (previously a new lesion noted at the time of targeted therapy resistance): Markedly diminished. A faint hypodense focus measuring approximately 0.4 cm is visible in segment IV; this is near the limit of reliable measurement on CT. Previously this measured 1.3 cm at the baseline immunotherapy scan. No peripheral enhancement is identified.

- Segment VI lesion (previously a new lesion noted at the time of targeted therapy resistance, measuring 0.9 cm): No discrete lesion is identified in segment VI on current examination. This lesion appears to have resolved or fallen below the threshold of CT detection.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The portal and hepatic veins are patent with normal enhancement.

Gallbladder: Normal-appearing gallbladder without gallstones or wall thickening. No pericholecystic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size and morphology with homogeneous enhancement. No pancreatic masses or ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fat stranding.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size (approximately 10.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension) and enhancement. No focal splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal nodules or masses. The right adrenal gland, previously unremarkable, remains so. No interval development of adrenal lesions.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size and enhancement with symmetric nephrographic phase enhancement. No hydronephrosis, hydroureter, or renal masses. No perinephric abnormality.

Retroperitoneum: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes identified. No soft tissue retroperitoneal masses.

Gastrointestinal tract: The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber without wall thickening or obstruction. Given the patient's recent history of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis with documented endoscopic and histologic involvement from the rectum to the splenic flexure, the large bowel wall is reviewed carefully. The sigmoid colon and descending colon demonstrate mild, diffuse mural thickening measuring up to approximately 4 mm — representing a notable improvement compared with what would be expected during the acute colitis episode, and more consistent with residual submucosal edema or post-inflammatory mucosal change rather than active acute inflammation. No pericolonic fat stranding or free air. These findings are substantially improved from the acute phase and are considered post-inflammatory in the appropriate clinical context.

Peritoneum and mesentery: No ascites. No peritoneal nodules or omental caking identified.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph nodes: The right external iliac lymph node, which previously measured 1.2 cm at the time of progression on targeted therapy and represented a new lesion at that time, now measures approximately 0.6 x 0.5 cm — reduced to within the borderline range and no longer confidently abnormal by size criteria. No other pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy is identified.

Soft tissues: No soft tissue masses or subcutaneous pelvic nodules. No pelvic floor abnormality.

Pelvic organs: The urinary bladder is normally filled and distended. The prostate gland is mildly prominent but within expected limits for the patient's age, without discrete suspicious lesions. No pelvic free fluid. Seminal vesicles appear normal.

Bony pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic lesions. Intact osseous structures.

---

NECK (CERVICAL SOFT TISSUES, partially included on scout and upper chest acquisition):
The lower cervical soft tissues are partially visualized. No grossly enlarged cervical or supraclavicular lymph nodes are identified on the portions imaged. Previously noted left cervical level II and III lymph nodes (up to 2.2 cm at staging, markedly decreased at first targeted therapy restaging) are not identified as discrete enlarged structures on the current examination.

---

BONE SURVEY (visualized osseous structures):
No lytic, sclerotic, or mixed osseous lesions are identified throughout the visualized skeleton including the spine, ribs, sternum, shoulder girdles, and pelvic bones. No pathologic fractures or periosteal reaction.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial interval decrease in the right axillary nodal mass, now measuring approximately 1.6 cm compared with 3.4 cm on the pre-immunotherapy baseline CT — a reduction of greater than 50% in the dominant nodal target lesion. The previously prominent central necrosis is no longer apparent.

2. Significant reduction in hepatic metastatic burden. The dominant segment VII hepatic lesion has decreased from 2.8 cm (pre-immunotherapy baseline) to approximately 1.2 cm. The segment II lesion has decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.7 cm. The previously new segment IV lesion has diminished markedly to a near-threshold 0.4 cm focus. The segment VI lesion, which measured 0.9 cm at the pre-immunotherapy baseline, is no longer identifiable on the current examination.

3. Complete resolution of previously documented subcutaneous nodular disease on the trunk and extremities. No measurable subcutaneous lesions are identified.

4. The right external iliac lymph node has decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.6 cm, now borderline normal in size.

5. No new suspicious lesions identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No new lymphadenopathy.

6. Mild residual large bowel wall thickening of the descending colon and sigmoid, compatible with post-inflammatory change in the setting of recently documented and treated grade 3 immune-mediated colitis; substantially improved from the acute phase, without active signs of colitis.

7. No pulmonary nodules. No mediastinal adenopathy. No adrenal, splenic, pancreatic, or new osseous lesions.

Overall, the findings demonstrate a meaningful and broad-based treatment response to immunotherapy across all previously identified disease sites, with reduction in hepatic lesion size and number, near-disappearance of soft tissue nodular disease, and decrease in the dominant right axillary nodal mass. These findings are consistent with an ongoing and deepening response to immunotherapy. No radiographic evidence of progressive disease.
